archetypes.name: 🗺️ All Journey Archetypes Browse all journeys

keyMyths.title: The Exodus
keyMyths.description: The Israelites' journey from slavery in Egypt to the Promised Land, led by Moses, represents a sacred pilgrimage towards freedom and divine covenant. It is a journey fraught with trials, testing faith and ultimately leading to the establishment of a new religious and national identity.

keyMyths.title: The Hajj
keyMyths.description: The Hajj is an annual Islamic pilgrimage to Mecca, Saudi Arabia, a mandatory religious duty for Muslims who are physically and financially capable of undertaking the journey. Pilgrims circumambulate the Kaaba, pray, and perform rituals that commemorate the trials of Abraham and his family, seeking spiritual purification and closeness to God.

keyMyths.title: The Shikoku Pilgrimage
keyMyths.description: The Shikoku Pilgrimage in Japan involves traversing a circuit of 88 temples associated with the Buddhist monk Kōkai (Kūkai), also known as Kōbō-Daishi. Pilgrims undertake this journey for spiritual purification, healing, and to connect with the teachings and presence of Kōbō-Daishi.
keyMyths.source: Shikoku Henro (Various pilgrimage guides and historical accounts)

keyMyths.title: The Journey of Gilgamesh
keyMyths.description: Gilgamesh's quest for immortality after the death of Enkidu can be interpreted as a sacred pilgrimage, albeit one driven by personal grief and fear rather than religious devotion. His journey to find Utnapishtim, the survivor of the flood, takes him to the edges of the world and through perilous trials, ultimately leading to a deeper understanding of mortality.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of El Dorado
keyMyths.description: While primarily driven by the lust for gold, the expeditions in search of El Dorado, the mythical lost city of gold in South America, can also be seen as a misguided sacred pilgrimage. The conquistadors ventured into the unknown, facing hardship and death, in pursuit of a legendary place of immense wealth, driven by a belief in its existence and the promise of riches.
keyMyths.source: Accounts of Spanish Conquistadors (e.g., journals of Gonzalo Jiménez de Quesada)

sources.name: Victor Turner, Dramas, Fields, and Metaphors: Symbolic Action in Human Society
sources.description: Explores the concepts of liminality and communitas, which are central to understanding the social and psychological aspects of pilgrimage.
sources.title: Victor Turner, Dramas, Fields, and Metaphors: Symbolic Action in Human Society

sources.name: Mircea Eliade, The Sacred and the Profane: The Nature of Religion
sources.description: Provides a foundational understanding of the distinction between the sacred and the profane, crucial for analyzing the motivations and experiences of pilgrims.
sources.title: Mircea Eliade, The Sacred and the Profane: The Nature of Religion

sources.name: Jonathan Z. Smith, To Take Place: Toward Theory in Ritual
sources.description: Examines the role of place and space in ritual practices, including pilgrimage, and how these spaces are constructed and imbued with meaning.
sources.title: Jonathan Z. Smith, To Take Place: Toward Theory in Ritual

sources.name: Eusebius, The History of the Church
sources.description: Provides historical accounts of early Christian pilgrimages to holy sites in Palestine, offering insights into the development of pilgrimage traditions.
sources.title: Eusebius, The History of the Church

sources.name: James Frazer, The Golden Bough
sources.description: While outdated in some aspects, this work offers a broad comparative study of myths and rituals, including those related to journeys and quests for spiritual knowledge.
sources.title: James Frazer, The Golden Bough
